result.question:
Competitive positioning scan for our splice-neoepitope prediction pipeline. Our pipeline (context): a modernized RNA-Seq cancer neoepitope pipeline that identifies tumor-specific splice junctions (HISAT2/STAR + regtools), filters them at the JUNCTION level against matched normal to keep only tumor-exclusive unannotated junctions, assembles junction-spanning contigs, translates candidate ORFs, scores MHC class-I PRESENTATION with MHCflurry 2.x Class1PresentationPredictor, optionally runs TCRdock structural validation of TCR-pMHC complexes, and is building an immunogenicity registry/calibrator (predicted->measured T-cell immunogenicity). It is packaged with Snakemake + conda, currently local-CPU-only at chr22 scale.

Research question: How do comparable cancer-neoepitope prediction pipelines and tools -- both splice/RNA-derived-neoantigen tools (e.g. NeoSplice, IRIS, SNAF/SplAdder-based tools, ASNEO) and the broader general neoepitope pipelines they share the field with (e.g. pVACtools/pVACseq, NeoPredPipe, antigen.garnish, MuPeXI, NeoFuse for fusions) -- that were published or substantially updated in 2024-2026 structure themselves, and where does our pipeline sit competitively?

For each comparable tool, characterize: (1) which neoepitope SOURCE(s) it handles (alternative splicing, SNV/indel, gene fusions, intron retention, ERV/TE, PTM); (2) its PRESENTATION layer -- MHC class I and/or class II, and which predictor (NetMHCpan, MHCflurry, BigMHC, TransPHLA, etc.); (3) whether it does any IMMUNOGENICITY or T-cell validation vs stopping at binding/presentation; (4) whether it does any TCR-pMHC STRUCTURAL modeling (this appears rare -- verify); (5) tumor/normal handling and how it defines "tumor-specific"; (6) PACKAGING/reproducibility (Snakemake, Nextflow, container, web server).

Then synthesize a POSITIONING assessment: our genuine differentiators (splice-junction focus with matched-normal junction-LEVEL filtering, presentation-likelihood scoring, TCRdock structural validation, the immunogenicity registry) versus our real gaps (class-I-only presentation, single-source, local-CPU scale), and which 2024-2026 trends we are with or behind. Prioritize peer-reviewed tools and their papers/repos as primary sources; note recency and maintenance status of each tool.
result.summary: Across the 2024-2026 comparable landscape, our pipeline sits in a crowded but differentiable niche. Two direct splice-neoantigen competitors bracket it: SNAF (Science Transl Med 2024) is the closest peer - splice-junction-first, class-I presentation via NetMHCpan/MHCflurry, plus a DeepImmuno immunogenicity layer and a BayesTS Bayesian tumor-specificity ranker - but it filters against a large GTEx/TCGA normal-tissue reference rather than a single matched normal, adds a B-antigen surface-protein path, and does NO TCR-pMHC structural modeling. splice2neo (2024) and NeoSplice (2022) are splice-only but shallower, stopping at peptide/ORF annotation or class-I binding + mass-spec presentation respectively. The general multi-source pipelines (pVACtools/pVACseq/pVACview, OmniNeo) are broader and further ahead on presentation: both score class I AND class II and integrate off-the-shelf immunogenicity predictors (BigMHC_IM, DeepImmuno, OmniNeo-CNN), and OmniNeo (2025, Nextflow) is the one tool that also does TCR-pMHC structural modeling (TCRmodel2 + PyMOL). Our genuine differentiators are the matched-normal junction-LEVEL filtering, presentation-likelihood scoring, and especially the TCRdock structural validation + measured-immunogenicity registry/calibrator (a step past the field's off-the-shelf immunogenicity models); our real gaps versus 2024-2026 trends are class-I-only presentation (peers increasingly do I+II), single-source (splice only, while pVACtools/OmniNeo are multi-source), and local-CPU chr22 scale.

findings.claim: SNAF (Science Transl Med 2024) is our closest direct competitor: splice-junction-first from RNA-seq, scoring class-I presentation via NetMHCpan/MHCflurry, then layering a DeepImmuno sequence-based immunogenicity step and a BayesTS Bayesian tumor-specificity ranker - the same presentation-plus-immunogenicity architecture as ours.
findings.evidence: SNAF's staged pipeline (Neojunction -> Translation -> MHC Presentation via netMHCpan/MHCflurry -> Immunogenicity via DeepImmuno) is documented verbatim in the STM 2024 paper and the frankligy/SNAF repo. Peer-reviewed, actively maintained (v0.7.0). Uses AltAnalyze junction quantification rather than our HISAT2/STAR+regtools, but the design-level splice-first architecture matches.

findings.claim: SNAF defines tumor-specificity by filtering junctions against a large normal-tissue reference (GTEx >2500 samples across >54 tissue types plus TCGA paratumor controls) rather than the single matched-normal RNA-seq sample our pipeline uses - a broader normal panel but not paired.
findings.evidence: SNAF tutorial docs state verbatim 'GTEx database containing >2500 samples spanning over 54 tissue types' plus 'TCGA paratumor database', and the STM paper confirms comparison against a MultiPath-PSI normal database (GTEx+TCGA). SNAF additionally layers BayesTS on top. Contrast with our per-patient matched-normal, junction-level filtering is a real design axis difference.

findings.claim: SNAF does NO TCR-pMHC structural modeling - its most advanced steps are sequence-based DeepImmuno immunogenicity and BayesTS ranking (plus a non-structural sequence/ML TCRbeta-integration module) - leaving our TCRdock structural validation a genuine differentiator against the closest competitor.
findings.evidence: Direct README fetch and paper confirm no AlphaFold/TCRdock/docking/3D structural modeling. SNAF does integrate TCR data via a sequence/ML module (pHLA-TCR binding), which is not structural, so TCRdock remains a differentiator.

findings.claim: SNAF covers both T-cell (MHC-bound) neoantigens and B-cell (altered surface protein) antigens - a broader antigen scope than our class-I-presentation-only pipeline.
findings.evidence: SNAF repo states verbatim it can 'predict, prioritize and visualize MHC-bound neoantigen for T cell (T antigen) and altered surface protein for B cell (B antigen)'. The B-antigen surface-protein path is a distinct antigen axis we do not address.

findings.claim: splice2neo (Bioinformatics Advances 2024) is a splice-only tool - alternative 3'/5' splice sites, exon skipping, intron retention, exitrons, mutually exclusive exons - that stops at peptide/ORF sequence annotation with NO MHC presentation, immunogenicity, or TCR-pMHC layer, making it strictly shallower than our pipeline downstream of junction detection.
findings.evidence: Primary repo + peer-reviewed paper (vbae080, PMID 38863673) confirm the five splice-event classes and that final outputs are peptide-context sequences/ORF annotations; the README explicitly states it does not include MHC binding prediction, immunogenicity scoring, or TCR-pMHC modeling. It links junctions to causal somatic SNV/indels as inputs but does not treat those as neoepitope sources; no fusions/ERV/PTM.

findings.claim: NeoSplice (Bioinformatics Advances 2022) is splice-only (validated on exon skipping, partial exon loss, partial intron gain), class-I-only via NetMHCpan 4.0 with a 500 nM cutoff, and validates with mass-spec immunopeptidome presentation only - no functional T-cell/immunogenicity testing and no TCR-pMHC structural modeling.
findings.evidence: Paper (PMID 35669345) confirms NetMHCpan 4.0 8-11mer, <500 nM, and MS-only validation (4/37 = 10.8% recovered from K562.A2 immunopeptidome). Repo README confirms class-I-only ('future plans to expand to MHC-II'). Authors note extension to SNV/indel/fusion is possible but unimplemented; a 2022 tool, older than the 2024-2026 window.

findings.claim: pVACtools/pVACseq/pVACview is a general MULTI-SOURCE neoepitope pipeline (SNV, indel/frameshift, gene fusions via pVACfuse, splicing via pVACsplice) - broader in source coverage than our splice-only pipeline.
findings.evidence: pVACtools suite genuinely spans pVACseq (SNV/indel from VEP VCF), pVACfuse (fusions from BEDPE), pVACsplice (cis-splicing variants); pVACview is the shared prioritization UI (Genome Medicine 2024). Note: the survived claim that pVACsplice uses RegTools junctions from paired WES/RNA was REFUTED (1-2) - pVACsplice targets cis-splicing variants altered by somatic SNVs, mechanistically different from our direct RNA-seq junction detection, so the multi-source contrast holds but the two splice approaches are not identical.

findings.claim: pVACtools scores BOTH MHC class I (up to 8 algorithms incl. MHCflurryEL, NetMHCpanEL, BigMHC-EL) and class II (NetMHCIIpanEL), whereas our pipeline is class-I-only via MHCflurry Class1PresentationPredictor - a real presentation-layer gap for us.
findings.evidence: pVACtools v6 paper states presentation scoring for class I via MHCflurryEL/NetMHCpanEL/BigMHC-EL and class II via NetMHCIIpanEL; Genome Medicine paper cites 'up to 8 binding algorithm scores for class I ... up to 4 for class II'. Actively maintained (v6.x/v7.0.x, 2026). Our class-I-only status is confirmed in project CLAUDE.md.

findings.claim: pVACtools goes beyond binding/presentation to explicit class-I immunogenicity prediction (BigMHC_IM, DeepImmuno) integrated into candidate prioritization - the same immunogenicity axis our registry/calibrator targets, but pVACtools uses off-the-shelf pre-trained predictors rather than a measured-immunogenicity calibrator.
findings.evidence: v6 paper: BigMHC_IM predicts which presented peptides trigger T-cell expansion; DeepImmuno-CNN scores pMHC immunogenicity. Corroborated by pVACtools docs listing both as class-I Immunogenicity tools. These are pre-trained off-the-shelf models, distinguishing them from our registry-fed calibrator that maps predicted->measured T-cell immunogenicity.

findings.claim: OmniNeo (Frontiers in Immunology 2025, Nextflow) is the one comparable tool that matches nearly our full downstream stack AND does TCR-pMHC structural modeling: multi-source (SNV/indel/frameshift/fusion/non-coding), class I AND class II presentation (NetMHCpan v4.1 + NetMHCIIpan v4.0), a CNN immunogenicity model (OmniNeo-CNN, AUC 0.88), and TCRmodel2 + PyMOL structural modeling/optimization of the TCR-pMHC complex.
findings.evidence: Paper confirms NetMHCpan v4.1 (8-11mer HLA-I) + NetMHCIIpan v4.0 (15-18mer HLA-II); OmniNeo-CNN trained on 10,834 peptides (AUC 0.88/AUPR 0.81 on a 20% held-out split); and TCR modeling via iTCep/GRATCR CDR3beta generation + TCRmodel2 3D structure + PyMOL. Structural modeling is framed as an application/demonstration case (Case 3), not a mandatory core step. This is the strongest 2025 competitor and the tool most overlapping our TCRdock differentiator - though it uses TCRmodel2 rather than TCRdock and immunogenicity metrics are self-reported.

findings.claim: NeoPredPipe (BMC Bioinformatics 2019) handles only SNV/indel neoantigens (no splicing, no fusions), is class-I-focused via netMHCpan, and estimates T-cell recognition via the sequence/alignment-based Luksza recognition potential (A x R) - computational, not measured immunogenicity.
findings.evidence: Repo README + BMC Bioinformatics 2019 paper confirm VCF/ANNOVAR SNV+indel handling only, no fusion/splice detection. Recognition potential = amplitude A (WT-vs-mutant binding-probability ratio) x R (pathogen-peptide similarity), the Luksza 2017 model - alignment/sequence-based. NOTE: the survived claim that its presentation layer is 'class I only via netMHCpan on 8-10mers' was REFUTED (0-3); treat class specifics cautiously. NeoPredPipe is an SNV/indel general tool, not a splice competitor, and is older than the window.

findings.claim: ImmuneApp (Nature Communications 2024) is an HLA class-I-only presentation/epitope predictor (CNN+LSTM+attention, trained on 349,650 ligands, with BA/EL/AP sub-models) - a component-level predictor rather than an end-to-end neoantigen pipeline, illustrating the 2024 deep-learning presentation-predictor trend our MHCflurry layer competes against.
findings.evidence: Nature Comms 2024 (PMID 39414796) + repo confirm HLA-I-only, interpretable attention-based CNN+LSTM hybrid, 349,650 ligands, and three sub-models ImmuneApp_BA/EL/AP for binding affinity/ligand probability/antigen presentation. It is a predictor building block, not a source-to-immunogenicity pipeline like ours.

findings.claim: POSITIONING: Our genuine differentiators are (a) matched-normal junction-LEVEL filtering (peers use large normal-tissue reference panels e.g. SNAF's GTEx, not paired per-patient junction filtering), (b) presentation-likelihood scoring, (c) TCRdock structural TCR-pMHC validation (absent in SNAF/splice2neo/NeoSplice/pVACtools; only OmniNeo has a structural step, via TCRmodel2), and (d) a measured-immunogenicity registry/calibrator that maps predicted->measured T-cell immunogenicity (a step past the field's off-the-shelf DeepImmuno/BigMHC_IM/OmniNeo-CNN predictors).
findings.evidence: Synthesis across confirmed per-tool findings: no comparable tool combines all four. TCRdock structural validation is shared only with OmniNeo (different modeler); the measured-immunogenicity calibrator direction is unmatched (all competitors use pre-trained immunogenicity models, not a registry-fed calibrator). Confidence medium because the differentiation is a cross-tool inference, not a single-source fact, and the calibrator is still 'being built' per our own context.

findings.claim: POSITIONING: Our real gaps versus 2024-2026 trends are (a) class-I-only presentation while peers increasingly do class I AND II (pVACtools NetMHCIIpanEL, OmniNeo NetMHCIIpan v4.0), (b) single-source (splice only) while the leading general pipelines are multi-source (pVACtools: SNV/indel/fusion/splice; OmniNeo: SNV/indel/frameshift/fusion/non-coding), and (c) local-CPU chr22 scale while comparators ship container/workflow-managed genome-scale packaging (OmniNeo Nextflow, pVACtools Docker).
findings.evidence: Class-II support is confirmed for pVACtools (claim 12/15) and OmniNeo (claim 17); multi-source coverage confirmed for both (claims 14, 18-context). Our class-I-only and local-CPU-chr22 status is from project context. Confidence medium because packaging-scale comparison rests partly on our own self-described current state rather than a benchmarked comparison, and Snakemake+conda packaging is itself reproducible-competitive even if scale is currently limited.

result.caveats: Time-sensitivity: pVACtools (v6/v7, 2026) and SNAF/OmniNeo (2024-2025) are actively maintained and versioned; specific predictor rosters and version pins will drift, so any 'up to N algorithms' figure is a snapshot. NeoSplice (2022) and NeoPredPipe (2019) predate the 2024-2026 window and appear less actively maintained - they are context, not current frontier. Two survived claims were REFUTED on re-vote and should not be relied on: (1) that pVACtools' pVACsplice uses RegTools junctions from paired WES/RNA the way we do (1-2) - pVACsplice targets cis-splicing SNV-driven variants, a different mechanism; and (2) that NeoPredPipe's presentation is definitively class-I-only via netMHCpan on 8-10mers (0-3). Weak-evidence spots: several immunogenicity performance metrics (OmniNeo-CNN AUC 0.88) are self-reported from the tool's own paper on an internal held-out split, not external validation; the pVACtools multi-predictor count (claim 15) and NeoSplice class-I designation (claim 10) were split 2-1 votes resting partly on repo docs rather than paper body. The two positioning-synthesis findings are cross-tool inferences (medium confidence), not single-source facts, and depend partly on our own self-described current state (class-I-only, chr22, calibrator still being built). This scan did not systematically cover IRIS, ASNEO, antigen.garnish, MuPeXI, or NeoFuse - only SNAF, splice2neo, NeoSplice, pVACtools, OmniNeo, NeoPredPipe, and ImmuneApp were verified.
openQuestions: How does our matched-normal junction-LEVEL filtering compare empirically (sensitivity/false-positive rate for tumor-exclusive calls) against SNAF's large-panel GTEx/TCGA reference approach - is per-patient pairing actually more specific, or does it under-power on low-support junctions?
Does OmniNeo's TCRmodel2-based structural step outperform or underperform our TCRdock/AlphaFold path on TCR-pMHC complex accuracy, and should we benchmark against or adopt TCRmodel2?

What is the realistic delta between our measured-immunogenicity registry/calibrator and off-the-shelf immunogenicity models (DeepImmuno, BigMHC_IM, OmniNeo-CNN) - does calibration on measured T-cell data produce a demonstrable accuracy gain that justifies the differentiator claim?

The scan omitted IRIS, ASNEO, antigen.garnish, MuPeXI, and NeoFuse - do any of these (esp. IRIS for splicing or antigen.garnish for class-II/immunogenicity) occupy positioning space that changes our competitive gaps, particularly on class-II presentation?

refuted.claim: pVACtools v6 added a dedicated splicing module, pVACsplice, that predicts neoantigens from tumor-specific alternative splicing junctions identified by RegTools from paired whole-exome and RNA sequencing data - the same RegTools-based junction source our pipeline uses.
NeoPredPipe's presentation layer is MHC class I only, using netMHCpan for binding prediction across 8-, 9-, or 10-mers.
